_meta.purpose: Remote-work tax residency checker (task 2) — Phase 1 data. The 'convenience of the employer' states: states that tax a nonresident remote worker as if every day were worked at the employer's in-state office, which can create double taxation with the worker's home state. Reciprocity and credit-for-taxes-paid live in the existing src/data/reciprocity.ts — not duplicated here.
_meta.phase: 1 — data, pending owner sign-off. Confirm each state against its Department of Revenue before Phase 2.
_meta.schema: code -> { name, applies, nuance, employer_necessity_exception, source, source_url, verify_status }

NY.name: New York
NY.nuance: The oldest and most aggressive convenience rule. Taxes a nonresident's wages as NY-source unless the remote work is for the employer's necessity, not the employee's convenience.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.nuance: Applies the convenience rule to nonresident remote workers of PA employers.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.nuance: Applies a convenience rule to nonresident employees of Delaware employers.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.nuance: Applies a convenience rule to nonresident remote workers.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.nuance: Amended 2024: the convenience rule applies only after at least 7 days of physical presence in Nebraska in the year. If the nonresident never sets foot in NE, it does not apply.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.nuance: Limited: CT applies its convenience rule only to nonresidents whose home state itself has a convenience rule (a reciprocal-retaliation design).

NJ.name: New Jersey
NJ.nuance: Enacted a convenience rule that applies only to nonresidents from states that impose their own convenience rule (mirrors CT). New Jersey publishes an FAQ on it.
NJ.source: NJ Division of Taxation — Convenience of the Employer Sourcing Rule FAQ (primary)

_owner_review_notes: NJ is confirmed against the state Division of Taxation FAQ (primary). The other six are from secondary summaries and need confirmation against each state DOR — convenience rules have changed recently (NE's 2024 amendment is the example).

Most convenience states offer an 'employer necessity' exception: if the employer requires the remote work for a genuine business reason (not employee convenience), the wages may not be re-sourced. The tool must surface this exception prominently, not just the rule.

This is a rules-map, not a tax computation — the tool outputs which rules apply and where double taxation risk sits, then leans on the existing reciprocity + credit-for-taxes-paid data for the states that have those instead.
